metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: introduction

page_content: every human being is vested with personal rights and his corresponding duties towards others. that is, a person has the right of not being harmed by others and at the same time, he has the duty not to harm others. infringement (violation) of one's right is called a wrong. as per law wrongs are classified as civil and criminal in nature.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: introduction

page_content: civil cases affect the loss to an individual and the victim in civil case has to approach the court on his own. criminal cases affect the authority of the state to maintain law and order, to protect the lives & properties of its citizens. the state is supposed to conduct investigation on behalf of the citizens and launch criminal prosecution against the offender. hence the police are expected to know the law of the land in detail to exercise their powers in preventing as well as solving a crime.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: what is criminal investigation?

page_content: investigation is nothing but collection of facts and circumstances of an incident which has been reported at a police station.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: crime/offence:

page_content: investigation begins after a crime has been committed. crime/offence can be a cognizable crime that is non-bailable, or a non-cognizable crime that is bailable. the classification of an offence as to whether it is a cognizable offence, non-cognizable offence, bailable offence or non- bailable offence and compoundable or non-compoundable - is listed in the first schedule of cr.p.c.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: powers and duties of police officer in investigation of crime:

page_content: the powers of police officers to conduct the investigation are dealt under sections 154-176 cr.p.c.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: powers and duties of police officer in investigation of crime:

page_content: 1. he has to receive all information without any discrimination from the complainants and to register cases on the cognizable offences. 2. he has to take-up investigation or to entrust investigation to his subordinate without any delay as per procedure. 3. he has to submit the original fir to the court and copies of fir to the superior officers, immediately after registration the case. 4. he has to collect all the evidence required to establish the offence. 5. he has to produce the arrested accused before the court within 24 hours of his arrest excluding the journey time. 6. he has to collect all the required documents and file along with charge sheet.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: the following are the core skills of a criminal investigator:

page_content: 1. curiosity: having curiosity involves asking questions to discover the reasons for the occurrence of a particular event. curiosity can help identify new sources to retrieve information for investigation. 2. problem-solving: being a problem solver helps investigators compile the information they gather. a majority of investigation work involves solving a case based on the series of events. 3. research skills: when investigating, it is helpful to know how to collect relevant information from different sources. for instance, an investigator may research the background of perpetrators from official records and previous accomplices to learn about their criminal history etc. 4. evidence collection: evidence collection is the ability to find relevant facts that can help understand why something may have occurred. 5. interviewing skills: interviewing skills are essential to interrogate persons and gather useful information from them. 6. report writing : knowledge of how to prepare a report allows investigators to organise and document the information properly and submit their findings to court to convict or acquit an individual. 7. critical thinking : critical thinking is useful in situations when you have all the information and you need to connect them to arrive at the conclusion. 8. flexibility: to accept changing circumstances, it is essential that investigators are flexible and willing to adapt themselves to new and unexpected situations. 9. observation: another skill necessary to be an investigator is to be attentive to one's surroundings. whether you are observing a suspect, a scene of a crime or a place of interest, your observation skills can help you gather information and infer conclusions faster.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

metadata.heading: first information report

page_content: the officer in charge of a police station is vested with the powers to conduct investigation as per cr.p.c. it starts with fir, which sets the criminal law in motion. firs can be broadly classified into four categories;

metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: first information report

page_content: 1. information true in all aspects. 2. information partly true. 3. information true in respect of the offence but not the involvement of the accused cited. 4. information which is totally false.

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: false information is usually of two types:

page_content: - (a) where no offence has taken place at all and, - (b) where an offence has taken place but the offenders are different from those cited in the fir

metadata.heading: first information report

page_content: fir legal provisions : (sections 154,155,156 (3) of criminal procedure code), pso nos.409 to 416)

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: importance of fir:

page_content: the first information is that information which is given to the police first in point of time relating to the commission of a cognizable case.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) oral/written information:

page_content: on receiving the written information in regard to a cognizable offence from the complainant, the sho has to register fir basing on the contents of the information and take up investigation.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: the information when given orally:

page_content: when information is given orally, it can be written down by any officer on the direction of the officer in charge of a police station. the fir shall be sent forthwith to the magistrate having jurisdiction besides superior police officers (sec. 157 cr.p.c). delay in lodging the fir or in sending it to the magistrate must be satisfactorily explained. otherwise it goes against the prosecution.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) sms/e-mail/telephonic:

page_content: sometimes information regarding the cognizable offence may be intimated to the officer in charge of a police station by means of a telephonic or telegraphic or sms or e-mail messages. such information does not constitute a valid first information report, since it is not possible to obtain the signature of the informant.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: when can a telephonic message be regarded as fir?

page_content: if the telephonic or telegraphic/sms/e-mailmessage is given by known person who discloses his identity and it contains all the required facts which can constitute a cognizable offence and is reduced into writing by officer in charge of police station it can be treated as fir.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) endorsements from nhrc/shrc/courts/senior o fficers:

page_content: if the sho receives a petition endorsed by the nhrc or shrc or courts or senior officers with instructions to register a case and do investigation on a cognizable offence or non-cognizable offence by the courts, it should be registered. the io should complete investigation and submit final report.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) reports of other departments/hospital:

page_content: if the sho receives a petition or intimation on a cognizable offence from other departments it should be registered as fir. the io should complete investigation and submit final report.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (e) suo - motto:

page_content: suo-moto, meaning on its own motion, is a latin legal term used where a government agency acts on its own cognizance.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (e) suo - motto:

page_content: some times the sho receives information about the occurrence of an illegal act or possession of some prohibited material. he will go to the place and catch the accused, seize the material before the panch witnesses and basing on the panchanama, he will register a case against the accused. this type of fir is called as suo-moto fir.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (f) paper news / electronic media reports:

page_content: if the sho received information in regard to a cognizable offence through paper news or electronic media reports, he has to make a gd entry and verify the facts and if it is cognizable offence, he has to register a case.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: zero fir/other than jurisdictional fir:

page_content: as per sec 154 of criminal procedure code, every police officer is law bound to register the first information report of any cognizable offence committed, irrespective of the jurisdiction in which the offence was committed. if it is not committed in his jurisdiction, he registers the fir under number 00 and sends it to the police station, where the offence was committed for further investigation. such fir is called as zero fir. it is for the convenience of the victim that the case is recorded and sent to the concerned police station.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: mandatory registration of fir-supreme court guidelines:

page_content: a constitution bench of the supreme court in lalita kumari v. govt.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: mandatory registration of fir-supreme court guidelines:

page_content: of u.p [w.p.(crl) no; 68/2008] held that registration of first information report is mandatory under section 154 of the code of criminal procedure if the information discloses commission of a cognizable offence and no preliminary inquiry is permissible in such a situation.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: mandatory registration of fir-supreme court guidelines:

page_content: if the information received does not disclose a cognizable offence but indicates the necessity for an inquiry, a preliminary inquiry may be conducted only to ascertain whether cognizable offence is disclosed or not.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: mandatory registration of fir-supreme court guidelines:

page_content: - (i) the police officer cannot avoid his duty of registering offence if cognizable offence is disclosed. action must be taken against erring officers who do not register the fir if information received by them discloses a cognizable offence. - (ii) a preliminary inquiry should not exceed 7 days. the fact of such delay and the causes of it must be reflected in the general diary entry.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 155 cr.p.c.

page_content: procedure to be followed in case of information received in police station about the commission of a non-cognizable offence

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 155 cr.p.c.

page_content: there are two procedures:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: on receipt of petition/complaint of offence of non-cognizable in nature directing the complainant to go to court.

page_content: - whenever sho receives a petition/complaint containing the contents of offence non-cognizable in nature, should make entry in the general diary mentioning the contents of the petition in brief. - sho should explain to the complainant that police cannot investigate a case non-cognizable in nature and he has to seek - redressal in court of law. - sho must inform the same in writing to the petitioner.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: on receipt of petition/complaint of offence of non-cognizable in nature directing the complainant to go to court.

page_content: procedure to be followed to obtain permission from the court to taking up investigation in case sho receives petition/complaint containing the contents of non-cognizable offence.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: whenever sho receives such petition/complaint:

page_content: - the sho should make entry in the general diary mentioning the contents of the petition in brief. - he will make an endorsement on the original petition. - mentioning the date and time of receipt of petition - also mention about the commission of offences under noncognizable sections of offence (mention the sections specifically).

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: whenever sho receives such petition/complaint:

page_content: - and also write on the petition that the magistrate concerned is being requested to accord permission to investigate the case as per the provisions of section 155(2) cr.p.c. - prepare a report addressing the area judicial magistrate under section 155(2) cr.p.c. for according permission to investigate the information relating to non-cognizable offence and send it to court by enclosing copy of the petition.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: on receipt of permission proceedings from the magistrate to investigate a non-cognizable case...

page_content: - sho has to make entry in the gd about the receipt of proceedings. - sho should endorse on the original petition mentioning the date and time of receipt of proceedings of the magistrate. - and also mention case crime no. and section of law and taking up the investigation. - sho has to issue fir on the original petition by allotting the regular crime no.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: salient points to be remembered while registering a fir:

page_content: - (a) write the fir immediately with all available details in the report/petition, and enter the brief of fact at once in the gd - (b) name of the complainant/informant, father's/husband's name, age, occupation and residence should be noted. - (c) the time of reporting at the police station and brief particulars of the report and the crime number and section of law and indicating the action taken. - (d) obtain the signature (if illiterate, the thumb impression) of the complainant or informant on all the pages of the statement. - (e) record on the original written statement of the informant, the date and hour of its receipt and through whom it was received. - (f) ascertain and incorporate in the fir the reasons for the delay, if any, in the receipt of the report or in the making of the complaint at the police station. - (g) place of occurrence and distance and direction from the police station. - (h) name and address of the accused (the full name of each accused with aliases, if any, address and father's name) - (i) details of property stolen/taken away, if any. if the list of stolen property is lengthy, it must be made on a separate sheet of a paper and attached to the fir and fact mentioned in this column with signatures of the both the complainant and the sho.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: salient points to be remembered while registering a fir:

page_content: in simple the complaint / report must cover the following 5'w's & 1 'h'.

metadata.heading: salient points to be remembered while registering a fir:

page_content: - 1- w - what happened? - 2- w - when did it happen? - 3- w - where did it happen? - 4- w - who possibly committed the crime? - 5- w - why it happened (motive) - 1- h - how was it committed?

page_content: the sho of a police station not below the rank of a constable can register the fir. he may be

metadata.heading: who can register fir:

page_content: - (a) head constable - (b) asst. sub-inspector - (c) sub-inspector - (d) inspector - (e) deputy superintendent of police or other senior officer.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: appropriate sections of law:

page_content: the sho has to register the case basing on the contents of report / complainant under appropriate sections of law. if there is any doubt he has to take advice from the senior officers or from the prosecuting officers. if case is not registered under appropriate section of law it may cause difficulty in investigation. thus the sho has to take care while registering the case.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: appropriate sections of law:

page_content: if delay has occurred in lodging the information by the informant or victim, the officer investigating the case should obtain explanation from the informant in regard to such delay and incorporate the same in the statement of the witnesses.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: grave fir/crimes: (pso-52-7)

page_content: the firs/crimes having gravity in nature are called as grave firs / crimes. these cases are to investigated by the inspector rank officer or above. in general these crimes are serious in nature and may affect the public peace and tranquility.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: grave fir/crimes: (pso-52-7)

page_content: the following classes of cases will be termed as grave firs/ crimes :

metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: grave fir/crimes: (pso-52-7)

page_content: - (a) dacoity - (b) high-way/house/ train robbery - (c) robbery over rs.1000/- - (d) murder - (e) all terrorist offences and those affecting security - (f) culpable homicide not amounting to murder - (g) rape

metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: grave fir/crimes: (pso-52-7)

page_content: - (h) house breaking and theft over rs.50, 000/- - (i) any disturbance, riot or affray of a specially grave nature - (j) theft over rs.1,00,000/- - (k) cases of counterfeit coins and currency notes - (l) organized crime of any description including kidnapping / abduction for ransom. - (m) cases of manufacture or use of bombs or explosives or arms and ammunition whether political or not - (n) assault on police officers, magistrates or judicial officers - (o) offences under sections 400 & 401 of the indian penal code - (p) conspiracy cases - (q) serious road accidents - (r) professional poisoning and drug trafficking, death or injury by noxious gases, cases arising due to food poisoning or consumption of adulterated liquor or spurious drugs. - (s) offences resulting in grievous hurt and serious mischief or arson committed on members of scs & sts on caste consideration - (t) attempted suicide or death in suspicious circumstances by young married women during the first 7 years of their marriage, trafficking in women and children - (u) cases of misappropriation involving property of value of rs.2 lakhs or more of government department or undertakings or financial institutions - banking or non-banking. - (v) major organized financial crimes affecting public and involving public money of over rs. 5 lakhs value

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: specially grave firs/crimes: (pso-55-2)

page_content: the grave crimes or other crimes which are having seriousness and disturb the peace and tranquility of large number of people are called as especially grave fir / crime. the sdpo / acp or above the rank of sdpo / acp officer has to do investigation in these cases and to send immediate report to the government.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: specially grave firs/crimes: (pso-55-2)

page_content: the following offences are called as specially grave firs / crimes:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: specially grave firs/crimes: (pso-55-2)

page_content: - (a) dacoity - (b) highway robbery (all robberies of railway passengers are regarded as highway robberies) - (c) murder of a sensational nature - (d) manufacture of counterfeit currency notes and coins - (e) trafficking in drugs (narcotics and psychotropic substances) and drug poisoning cases - (f) communal riots - (g) caste riots

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: specially grave firs/crimes: (pso-55-2)

page_content: - (h) atrocities involving arson, murder, rape, assault or molestation against scheduled castes and scheduled tribes - (i) disturbance, riot or affray of a specially grave nature - (j) disturbance involving the use of fire arms and or explosives - (k) misappropriation of public funds and property of value of rs. 10 lakhs and above - (l) case in which fire is opened by a public servant - (m) organized large-scale misappropriation, criminal breach of trust, cheating by bogus institutions, finance companies etc. - (n) organized crime (mafia type or syndicated crime) including kidnapping for ransom, gang wars etc. - (o) case of possession, use or manufacture of bombs or explosive devices - (p) abduction or kidnapping or trafficking in women and children in an organised manner - (q) assault on the police except of a very trivial nature - (r) case of death or grievous injury allegedly by a police officer whether in public or private capacity, or to have occurred to any person (including suicide) while in police custody, or alleged police torture. - (s) serious fire accident involving loss of properties worth rs.10 lakhs and above, or involving loss of human life or both. - (t) fast, hartal, strike, bandh, or any form of agitation, which attracts public attention, or disrupts normal life or movement of traffic or accompanied by violence. - (u) serious accident or natural calamity in which human lives are involved - (v) railway accident except where it is trivial - (w) terrorist violence - (x) case of sensational nature or of public importance

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: specially grave firs/crimes: (pso-55-2)

page_content: the sho/sub-inspector of police has to inform about the occurrence of the grave or especially grave crime/fir as soon as possible to the superior officers by quickest means and to take precautions to control the law & order situation in the area. the sho has to forward the grave crime fir/ especially grave crime fir to the court, superintendent of police/cp and to the district magistrate in later case with special messenger.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir whom to be sent

page_content: the copy of the fir should be dispatched or made available to the following officers by the earliest possible means:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir whom to be sent

page_content: - the area judicial magistrate (in original). - the district collector

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir whom to be sent

page_content: - superintendent of police - nhai (national high way authority of india) in case of grave accidents - dcrb (district crime record bureau) - sub divisional police officer - station file - the complainant or informant (free copy).

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: entries in the relevant records / forms:

page_content: after registration of fir the sho has to mention the information in the records of police station. these records are two categories. one is bodily offence records and second is property offence records.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) bodily offence records:

page_content: 1. general diary 2. fir index / book 3. sch part-i 4. sch part-iv ( village history sheet) 5. rowdy sheet (if the accused is having rowdy sheet) etc.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) property offence records:

page_content: 1. general diary 2. fir index/book 3. sch part - i 4. sch part - ii (jurisdiction map) 5. sch part - iv (village history sheet) 6. dc/kd/suspect sheet (if the offender is having one) 7. lli (loose leaf index) etc.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir index book and importance of the various columns:

page_content: a first information report book / index shall be maintained in form 96 (pso-no-630). the entries in the register should be continued from year to year till all the pages in a book are used. the classification as per instructions relating to part-i of station crime history should be entered in this register in cases where it is not entered in part-i. even though no separate columns are provided in this register the entries should be made in the remarks column.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir index book and importance of the various columns:

page_content: all cognizable offences and reports forwarded by courts on endorsement including non-cognizable cases shall be registered in the fir book.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir index book and importance of the various columns:

page_content: the information pertaining to following may also be registered in the pro-forma of the fir. however, the offences so registered will not be given fir number, but details shall be recorded in special registers maintained for this purpose.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir index book and importance of the various columns:

page_content: all cases of suicides and accidental deaths where inquest is conducted under section 174(3) cr.p.c.,accidental fires, missing of persons, missing cattle, etc, cases booked under sections 41, 107 to 110 and 102 cr.p.c.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir index book and importance of the various columns:

page_content: if the report is made at police outpost, the incharge shall record the statement of the informant and send it immediately to the police station for the registration after entering the substance in the outpost g.d. however if a written report is presented its gist will be entered in the outpost g.d. and the report will be sent to the police station for immediate registration.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: fir index book and importance of the various columns:

page_content: if the officer in charge of police station receives a report during his tour, he should send it to the station with due endorsement for registration. in the meanwhile he can commence investigation.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: case laws regarding registration of fir / some other explanations in regard to fir :

page_content: it is not necessary that a formal registration of a case should have been made before proceeding to the spot, in order to bring inquest proceedings within the ambit of investigation. any step taken by a police officer, pursuant to such information, towards detection of the said offence, would be part of such investigation, even though the formal registration of the fir takes place only thereafter. the object of inquest is only to ascertain whether a person has died under unnatural circumstances and if so, what the cause of death is, ( sambu das vs. st. of assam air 2010 sc 3300, st. of up vs. bhagwant kishore joshi air 1964 sc 221 , maha singh vs. state (delhi administration) air 1976 sc 449) .

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: case laws regarding registration of fir / some other explanations in regard to fir :

page_content: the element of delay in registering fir or sending fir to the magistrate by would not in any manner weaken the prosecution case. ( balram singh vs state of punjab air 2003 sc 2213 ). where the fir contained only a brief statement of events, the delay in sending the fir to court could not have been to concoct a false case against the accused.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: case laws regarding registration of fir / some other explanations in regard to fir :

page_content: in hallu and others v state of madhya pradesh , it is held that it is not the requirement of section 154 cr.p.c which speaks of an information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence given to an in-charge of a police station that the report must be given by a person who has knowledge of the incident and such report cannot be refused to be treated as f.i.r holding that it is based on hearsay evidence.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: case laws regarding registration of fir / some other explanations in regard to fir :

page_content: in golla jalla reddy v state of ap , it has been held that where oral complaint disclosing commission of a cognizable offence has been made and police investigation has started on its basis and later on a second report is made in writing, the written report has to be considered as statement under section 161 cr.p.c and not as f.i.r and it cannot be used for corroboration of evidence of information.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: case laws regarding registration of fir / some other explanations in regard to fir :

page_content: information received in regard to commission of a cognizable offence is not required to be preceded by a f.i.r. it is the duty of the state to protect the life of the injured.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: case laws regarding registration of fir / some other explanations in regard to fir :

page_content: intimation of accidents received from a medical officer can be treated as fir. (1968 m.l.j. (cr) 75 ap) an information given by a constable to the si at his house that an injured person is in a precarious condition and was taken to the hospital will have to be treated as the fir (1965 (2) cr.l.j. 762 guj.)

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: case laws regarding registration of fir / some other explanations in regard to fir :

page_content: an fir is a public document. however, it is not substantive evidence. it can be used only to corroborate or contradict the informant.

metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

metadata.heading: drafting of complaint of cognizable offence by the sho:

page_content: as per the provisions of 154 cr.p.c. the sho has to reduce in to writing the information received orally in regard to a cognizable offence from the complainant and to register a case basing on the contents of the information.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: hence the fir

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: what is a crime scene?

page_content: the place of occurrence of the crime where physical evidences and clues relating to the crime are available is called scene of offence/crime scene. it may be any place either indoor, outdoor or a vehicle which provides information on the victim and the suspect, and is used to reconstruct the crime.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: importance of crime scene:

page_content: as the part of the investigation the investigating officer should immediately reach the scene of crime, identify the available physical evidences and collect them.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: importance of crime scene:

page_content: basing on the nature of crime, clues team must be called in to collect physical evidences if necessary. any delay to collect physical evidences, leads to the (wiping out) of them and it will be very difficult for experts to give their opinion. it helps to establish corpus delicti (latin term meaning 'body of the crime'), linkage between the criminal, the victim and the scene of occurrence through exchange of clues.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) physical location of the crime:

page_content: physical location of the crime scene is the place where a crime has been committed and where physical evidence or clues of such a crime are found.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) physical location of the crime:

page_content: there may be one or more crime scenes. in addition to the original crime scene the area to an extent of 3 to 4 kms surrounding it must be searched for any objects or articles left by criminals or hidden stolen property with the help of dog squad.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) type of crime:

page_content: the io should observe the type of crime at the crime scene. there may be certain types of crime, which have no 'scene' at all. the io should observe whether the scene of offence is located indoor or outdoor and guard it. he should observe it thoroughly with the help of clues team, and collect the physical evidence like fingerprints, footprints, blood stains, threads or any other clues, left by the criminals on the scene.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) size of the crime scene:

page_content: the scene of crime is not confined within the limits of immediate location. it can extend to as much of its surrounding as per the circumstances for the commission of the crime. it includes the way of approach and retreat of the culprit. sometimes same crime may have both indoor and outdoor scene. for example in a murder scene there may be multiple crime scenes:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) size of the crime scene:

page_content: - (i) the place from where the person was kidnapped. - (ii) the place where he/she was confined. - (iii) the place where the initial assault commenced leading to death. - (iv) the place where the dead body was moved. - (v) the place where physical or trace evidence or parts of the body connected with the crime are found. - (vi) the vehicle used to transport the dead body.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) size of the crime scene:

page_content: the scene of offence must be observed in a proper way before commencing investigation. it enables to know the modus operandi of the crime.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: types of crime scene:

page_content: the crime scenes are classified broadly in to three types basing on the place of occurrence:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) outdoor crime scene:

page_content: the outdoor crime scene is the most vulnerable to loss, contamination, and deleterious change of physical evidence in a relatively short period of time. destruction or deterioration of evidence due to environmental conditions such as heat, cold, rain, and wind are problems associated with outdoor scenes. investigators who encounter a combination of an indoor and outdoor scene should give priority to processing the outdoor component.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) outdoor crime scene:

page_content: night time outdoor crime scenes are particularly problematic. whenever possible, outdoor crime scenes should be held and secured until daylight for processing.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) indoor crime scene:

page_content: evidence at an indoor scene is generally less susceptible to loss, contamination and deleterious change. indoor crime scenes are usually easier to secure and protect, and securing a scene can be as simple as closing a door.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: conveyance crime scene:

page_content: conveyance is defined as 'something that serves as a means of transportation.' it is important that the i.o. recognizes that physical evidence recovered from these scenes may extend well beyond the conveyance itself. for example, impression evidence, such as shoe or footprints in soil, may be found leading away from the scene, and property removed from the conveyance may be deposited or dropped as the perpetrator flees the scene.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: conveyance crime scene:

page_content: it is not difficult to locate the scene of occurrence, but sometimes criminals simulate a scene to mislead the investigators, to avoid suspicion and try to pass on the guilt to others, sometimes natural phenomena or stray animals may alter the scene of occurrence.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: conveyance crime scene:

page_content: crime scenes are further classifies in to below mentioned four types based on the number of locations of crime occurrence .

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: conveyance crime scene:

page_content: - (i) primary crime scene - (ii) secondary crime scene - (iii) tertiary crime scene - (iv) continuing crime scene - (i) primary crime scene: the original location of a crime or accident where a crime actually took place. sometimes the crime scene may extend to another crime scene. investigating officer should visit the secondary scene. - (ii) secondary crime scene: secondary crime scene means that actual crime took place at one point and continued in other place where the crime was finally committed by the offenders, an alternate location where additional evidence may be found. the crime scene includes the victim's home, suspect's vehicle and road between the suspect and victim locations. an offender enters a residence (primary crime scene) takes the daughter in another room to sexually assault her (secondary crime scene) transport the victim (car would be the tertiary crime scene) commit another crime at a distant location for instance murder and dump the body (fourth crime scene). - (iii) tertiary crime scene: tertiary crime scene means the scene where the offence was committed after primary and secondary scenes. ex. a person was kidnapped in primary scene, murdered in secondary scene and buried in another place. it is called as tertiary crime scene. - (iv) continuing crime scene: the crimes may have been committed and completed at a number of places. all the sites are crimes scenes, where evidence relating to the crime is found. this is called as continuing crime scene.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: who has to visit the crime scene? cr.p.c section 157:

page_content: - (a) investigating officer: it is important that the investigating officer and his team proceed to the scene of crime as expeditiously as possible. io looks at the scene of crime to find out how the crime was committed, the physical clues that are to be collected, packed and transported and the experts to be contacted or called to the scene of offence, what method of search to be employed for the collection of physical clues etc.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: who has to visit the crime scene? cr.p.c section 157:

page_content: - (b) forensic experts: the forensic scientist has special training and education in chemistry and biology that is applied to the recognition, identification, collection, and preservation of physical evidence. various materials collected from the scene or other places connected to the crime are sent to concerned experts for their opinion. a forensic scientist collects evidence, biological evidence etc., and does crime scene reconstruction. (section 45 iea). - (c) clues team / fpb: clues team works under the technical supervision of the director of tsfsl. similarly, it will function under the direct supervision of the sp/cp concerned.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: the clues team is:

page_content: - c - crime l -laboratories u -ultimatum e - evidence s - system.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: the clues team is:

page_content: one vehicle is provided exclusively for clues team, for its mobility with all scientific instruments for collection, observation and recording of physical evidences.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: staff of clues team:-

page_content: 1. technician 2. photographer/ videographer 3. finger prints expert 4. one home guard for assistance.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: main duties of clues team:-

page_content: - (i) collection of physical evidence. - (ii) recording the scene of offence (photograph and video graph). - (iii) proper packing and handing over of physical evidences to i.o.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: dog squad:

page_content: dogs are used not only in the investigation of crimes, but also, to identify hashish (ganja), explosives and landmines in airports, railway stations and in the meeting places of national leaders. dog can easily smell any scent, and has the capacity to resolve the secretions of sweat glands, of human beings, even in minute quantity i.e. microgram level. it can smell the scent of a substance buried under a depth of 5 feet. dogs are also used for identification of human beings.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: evidentiary value of dogs:

page_content: the clues given by dog must be briefly taken into consideration, for the purpose of investigation. but, it cannot be proved absolutely, that a person is a criminal, basing on the evidence provided by dog. the i.o must treat each and every act of the dog as a clue and treat the dog as informant, collect sufficient evidence, combine it with other evidences of experts and produce in court to prove the crime.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: processing of crime scene:

page_content: broadly there are three (3) basic and simple stages in properly processing the crime scene.those stages consist of 1) scene recognition 2) scene documentation and 3) evidence collection. proper methods, techniques, knowledge of resources and equipment, all are pertinent to evidence collection. proper handling and packaging of evidence by the io / expert are also important.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: crime scene protocol

page_content: each crime scene is different and may require a different approach to processing the scene. however there is a basic crime scene protocol that should be adhered to in all crime scenes. the protocol will be of five steps.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: interview of complainant and witness.

page_content: interview is the first step in processing a crime scene. io may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case. an interview is a conversation between the police men and a suspect/accused/complainant and witness or victim which is controlled somewhat by the police officers.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: examination of the scene of crime:

page_content: examine the crime scene as the second step in the protocol. examine the scene of offence to identify the possible items of evidence. identify point of entry and point of exit, and get the general layout of the crime scene.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: documentation:

page_content: the third step in the protocol involves creating a pictorial record of the scene as well as a rough sketch to demonstrate the layout of the crime scene and to identify the exact position of the deceased or other evidence within the crime scene. in the documentation stage there are simple steps to properly document the crime scene. sketching the scene, photography, observation notes and videography.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: steps in recording the crime scene:

page_content: 1. protection 2. photography 3. videography 4. drawing the sketch of the soc 5. drawing observation mahazar 6. lifting of finger and foot prints and other impressions 7. examination by forensic science experts. 8. recording statements of witness at the soc

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: preservation/collection analysis of evidence.

page_content: preservation of the clue in the crime scene is the most important task of the police. he should isolate the area of the crime by cordoning it off.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: preservation/collection analysis of evidence.

page_content: suitable containers like polythene or glass bottles, polythene bags, paper envelopes, aluminum foils, etc. and preservative substances should be kept ready for proper preservation of evidence.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: interrogation -suspected/accused:

page_content: this is the last step in the protocol. if the accused / suspect is found at the crime scene, he should be examined thoroughly to elicit evidence in regard to the motive of crime, names of accused, weapon used, manner in which the offence was committed. his confession should be recorded before mediators and the crime weapons etc seized.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: searching of crime scene-search patterns: pso 423-424.

page_content: the primary purpose of inspecting the scene of crime is to search for evidence left behind by the criminal while committing the crime, known as burglars' visiting cards. such evidence will be invariably present in all scenes of crime is based on the locard's principle of exchange.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) spiral method:

page_content: the search is conducted following a spiral pattern working from outside to inside towards the center or focal point (towards the body, safe or any other key feature) or vice versa, either clockwise or anti clockwise. it is an effective search pattern for single person search, with a very high probability of locating minute evidence like fingerprints, footprints, etc. also, there is not much likelihood of the evidence being destroyed by the investigator's movements.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: spiral method

page_content: this method is recommended for indoor scenes of crime. it can also conduct outdoor scenes of crime if the area to be searched is small. here the i.o makes an ever-widening circular movement in which he starts at the focal point of the scene. the search is conducted in a spiral pattern working from outside to inside towards the center or focal point vice versa either clockwise or anti clockwise.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) grid method:

page_content: grid method is a slight modification of the strip method. this method is useful for small areas especially indoors. the area is divided into grids. in this method a single person can search the entire area by moving at right angles, and covering all the squares of the grid.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) grid method:

page_content: grid method

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) grid method:

page_content: wheel search

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) grid method:

page_content: this method of search is employed to inspect large outdoor scenes. the entire area is covered by a circle. the searchers start from the centre and proceed along the radial. they may have to repeat the process several times depending on the size of the circle.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) strip search:

page_content: commonly used when searching a large area looking for a large object. several explorers will stand in one long line and all walk the same direction. stakes and string can also be used to create 'lanes' for which each explorer would be responsible.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (e) zone method:

page_content: if the crime scene is large and enough personnel are not available, it is advisable to divide or sub-divide the area into smaller zones and sub zones and use this method of search. the method is particularly useful for searching in details the enclosed areas where items such as almirahs, safes, etc. have to be carefully inspected, and for cars or carriages which may contain minute trace evidence, necessitating a thorough search of all nooks and corners.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (e) zone method:

page_content: zone method

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: sketching of the crime scene - sketch patterns (indooroutdoor) (pso- 423-424)

page_content: there are three methods of drawing of crime scene sketch in crime investigation. the io has to use the method basing on the nature of the crime scene.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: sketching of the crime scene - sketch patterns (indooroutdoor) (pso- 423-424)

page_content: - (a) coordinate method or baseline method : this method uses the principle of measuring the distance of an object from two fixed points. the fixed points are normally selected in such a way that they are straight and most of the evidence is located in the vicinity. the line joining these points is known as the baseline. in indoor scenes one side of a wall of the room where the crime occurred can be taken as the baseline. in outdoor scenes any two trees or other fixed points can be joined and taken as the baseline.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: sketching of the crime scene - sketch patterns (indooroutdoor) (pso- 423-424)

page_content: baseline or coordinate method

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: sketching of the crime scene - sketch patterns (indooroutdoor) (pso- 423-424)

page_content: - (b) triangulation method : this method is particularly suitable for outdoor locations. two or more fixed points should be located and they should be as widely separated as possible. the evidentiary item need not be very close to the fixed point but should be located by measuring along a straight line from the fixed points. - (c) cross-projection method : it is useful when the evidentiary items are on the ceiling, wall or elsewhere in an enclosed space. the walls, windows and doors in a cross projection sketch are drawn as though the walls are extended flat on the floor. the measurements are taken from any two wall corners or roof corners to the evidentiary item.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: sketching of the crime scene - sketch patterns (indooroutdoor) (pso- 423-424)

page_content: triangulation method

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: sketching of the crime scene - sketch patterns (indooroutdoor) (pso- 423-424)

page_content: cross projection method

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) sketching of the crime scene:

page_content: the io has to prepare the sketch of crime scene basing on the place of occurrence by using any one of the sketch methods explained above. sketches are of two types:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) sketching of the crime scene:

page_content: - (1) rough sketch of the scene of crime - (2) sketching the crime scene on scale

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) sketching of the crime scene:

page_content: the sketch is the first visual record of the condition of the crime scene. it portrays the crime scene and items within that scene that are of interest to the investigation. sketches properly prepared are useful during the interrogation of witnesses, in making notes in the case diaries and in presenting information to the court. the sketch complements the photographs and notes made while observing the crime scene.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) sketching of the crime scene:

page_content: some model sketchs of crime are given below.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: rough sketch to final

page_content: bsapp.com the investigating officer should make a rough sketch of the crime scene on the basis of his own observation. the sketch need not be drawn to scale, but should indicate accurate distances between objects, dimensions of areas and relative directions.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: rough sketch to final

page_content: the items to be included in the sketch and the number of copies should be determined at the outset. all sketches should cover the following aspects in the case of indoor scenes, the size of the window(s) and room(s) and in the case of outdoors the scene with reference to 'magnetic north'. the apparently visible items should be indicated by accurate measurement from at least two fixed points. the position of eyewitnesses and accused persons and places indicating all stains, footprints, tire or drag marks and other objects of victim or accused.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: rough sketch to final

page_content: the following information must invariably be mentioned on the sketch.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: rough sketch to final

page_content: - the 'investigator's full name, rank, address. - crime number, section of law, police station, and district - address of the crime scene, its position in a building, landmarks and compass direction - the visible items of physical evidence & critical features of the crime scene. - a legend to the symbols may be used to identify objects or points.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: rough sketch to final

page_content: the rough sketch once drawn should not be altered. a smooth or fair sketch should be prepared on the basis of the rough sketch. the smooth sketch also known as crime scene plan drawing is prepared as per a convenient scale and plan from the details of rough sketch. color drawing will be helpful to distinguish various objects or the features of an object.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: photography:

page_content: photography is admissible in evidence u/s 9 of the evidence act. photos of various physical evidences present in the scene of offence must be taken in sequence, to know how the crime was committed.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: photography:

page_content: a camera records all details accurately which may escape the mind. in all-important cases, photographs, video graphs and where possible laser impressions should be taken of the scene and of the objects on which any useful clues are found.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
