metadata.heading: photography:

page_content: - (i) entire scene of offence with permanent structures of that area must be photographed. - (ii) if the injuries on a dead body are convenient for identification, photograph must be taken along with the place of dead body in such a way as to identify the injuries. - (iii) the physical objects like weapons must be photo graphed along with their place of location. - (iv) finger prints, foot prints and signs of tools, must be photographed. - (v) in various cases, surroundings of the scene of offence must also be covere in photo.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: photography:

page_content: note: the name and address of the photographer, description of the camera, date and time of photography and condition of the light at that time must be mentioned in case diary and photos should be sent to court.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: videography:

page_content: it is valuable for showing an overview of the crime scene and should be considered in major cases. it refreshes the memory of those who were involved in processing the crime scene.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: videography:

page_content: video tapes are considered evidence. in certain crimes like arson, videography provides an overall view of the scene and allows investigators who could not be present at the scene to visualize the important pieces of evidence and their relationship to one another.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: videography:

page_content: all the precautions suggested earlier for photography should also be followed for these recordings.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: notes/panchanama/observation proceedings:

page_content: the officer making the search should take down accurate and detailed notes, supported by accurate sketches drawn to scale, showing the whole layout and the exact places where the articles, etc. were found. it is not sufficient to say that an article was in a certain room or on a particular table, but its exact position must be noted and, if necessary, an enlarged sketch of that portion of the scene must be drawn.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: notes/panchanama/observation proceedings:

page_content: note making of crime scene:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: notes/panchanama/observation proceedings:

page_content: guidelines for recording observation notes:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: notes/panchanama/observation proceedings:

page_content: - (i) method of recording : the notes should start with the time of receipt of first information. the notes can be recorded as and when the observations are made. - (ii) date, time and location : the time and date of arrival at crime scene and the details of its location, environmental conditions (smoke, rain, dusk, dawn, etc.) the names of persons present at the scene on arrival should be recorded. - (iii) description of the victim and clothing : the name, age, height, weight, complexion, color of hair, eyes, if possible, the date of birth of victim should be noted. outer garments should be described in terms of type of garment and colour. - (iv) wounds on the victim : the exact location of a wound or injury, its type, size and in the case of a bruise, its color. - (v) recognition of each significant item of evidence : the description of each significant piece of evidence found or identified with complete details, such as the time when it was found, the exact location, who found it, how it was marked, sealed and packed. - (vi) missing items : the purpose of this entry is to note items that should have been normally associated with the crime being investigated, but which are absent at the crime scene or on the victim or deceased. for example, an item of clothing missing from the victim's body that could not be located at the scene of crime and which, if found later from the suspect or elsewhere, should be useful in linking evidence and suspect or vice-versa. - (vii) miscellaneous : any other details not included in photographs and sketches such as condition of surroundings, the investigating officer can reorganize all these notes for preparing his case diary and charge sheet.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: notes/panchanama/observation proceedings:

page_content: panchanama should be written to record the scene of offence. it should be written in the presence of local respectable person. names of respectable person should be mentioned. distance and direction of the place of writing panchanama from the ps, crime number, and section of law, police station and name of the investigation officer along with other present police officers must be mentioned in this panchanama. these proceedings must be sent to the court.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: reconstructing the crime scene:

page_content: crime scene reconstruction is the process of determining or eliminating the events and actions that occurred at the crime scene. analysis of the crime scene pattern, the location and position of the physical evidence and the laboratory examination of the physical evidence involves systematic study of related information and the logical formulation of a theory.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: stages in reconstruction:

page_content: reconstruction is considered a scientific fact-gathering process. the following are the five separate stages commonly used in the process of reconstruction.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: stages in reconstruction:

page_content: 1. data collection 2. conjecture 3. hypothesis formulation 4. testing 5. theory formation

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: stages in reconstruction:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of evidence

page_content: evidence used to resolve a crime is of two types: oral evidence and physical evidence.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: physical evidence:

page_content: they are available in the scene of offence, with the criminal and victim:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: physical evidence:

page_content: 1. body fluids: blood, semen, saliva, sweat, tears, urine and feaces 2. parts of the body: skull, flesh, teeth, bones, nails, intestines and hair. 3. physical and chemical toxic substances and their containers. 4. documents and photos, video clippings, pens and inks. 5. guns, explosives and their leftovers. 6. cotton and woolen threads. 7. finger prints, foot prints, shoe prints. 8. glass pieces, dry paint chips. 9. signs of machines: signs of type writer, tyre marks. 10. signs of teeth and lips. 11. signs carved on metal articles: serial numbers. 12. soil and dust (occupational dust). 13. signs of tools. 14. implements: blades, screw driver, hammer, crowbar, spade, knives, dragons, bow and arrows. 15. substances related to plants & animals.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: physical evidence:

page_content: apart from the above, any other material, which shows link between the criminal and scene of offence, maybe utilized as physical evidence.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: trace evidence - biological fluids

page_content: trace evidence is invariably small in quantity whenever two objects come into mutual contact, each leaves trace evidence on the other. this trace may be distinct, faint or invisible depending on the nature of the object and place.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: trace evidence - biological fluids

page_content: whenever two people come in contact with each other, a physical transfer occurs. hair, skin, clothing fibers, pollen, glass fragments, any number of different types of material can be transferred from one person to another. these transferred materials constitute what is called trace evidence. small but measurable amounts of physical or biological material may be found at a crime scene.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: handling, lifting, and packing of physical evidences

page_content: the scene of offence must be preserved carefully until the arrival of finger prints and explosives and clues team members. physical evidence should be described in detail with full information as to when and where such evidence was found who found it, handled it and how it was disposed.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: handling, lifting, and packing of physical evidences

page_content: each item should be packed separately so as to avoid breakage, loss or contamination in transit. tweezers, forceps and similar tools are used to collect and place traces and small items into containers. documentary evidence should be first placed in transparent envelopes without folding or bending, and then placed between two pieces of rigid cardboard and packed in a suitable cloth-lined envelope cover. if the stain is on a solid object that can be moved, such as a knife, crowbar, fire arm or any other weapon, it is preferable to transport the whole object, protecting the area of the stain or completely enclosing the object in a package (if it is small enough).

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: handling, lifting, and packing of physical evidences

page_content: the specific areas of such fabric which contain trace evidence should be folded in such a way as to protect such areas to avoid loss of such materials by falling off, peeling off, etc. food and edible substances should be placed in as many separate moisture proof bottles or containers as necessary to avoid any contamination. while lifting and packing materials for dispatch to the fsl meticulous care should be taken to see that no article is inadvertently contaminated with extraneous matter or things likely to interfere in its examination.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: handling, lifting, and packing of physical evidences

page_content: physical evidence available in the scene should be collected in the presence of elderly persons of that area under a mahazar. integrity of the evidence, i.e original character of the evidence should be maintained while collecting it. the chain of custody of evidence collected should be recorded in detail. each item should be packed separately so as to avoid breakage, loss or contamination in transit.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: maintaining the chain of custody:

page_content: the term chain of custody means maintaining a record of every article of evidence, from the point of discovery at a crime scene to its collection and transport to the laboratory for examination and its temporary storage till it is finally submitted to the court. chain of custody assures continuous accountability. failure to substantiate the chain of custody may lead to serious questions regarding the authenticity and integrity of the evidence and the examination rendered upon it, making the whole process an exercise in futility.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: letter of advice:

page_content: the io has to forward the material collected from the scene of offence, victim and accued to the expert for opinion through a letter with proper authorisation. in the proforma prescribed for this purpose. this proforma is called letter of advice in form 54.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: letter of advice:

page_content: the forwarding letter should be sent in a separate sealed cover either by a special messenger or by a post.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: information to be mentioned in the letter of advice:

page_content: the following information / particulars must be mentioned, in the letter of advice:-

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: information to be mentioned in the letter of advice:

page_content: - (i) police station, crime number, section of law. - (ii) number of persons or animals. - (iii) how many died. - (iv) history of the case in brief, reasons for the case, when, how and where. - (v) list of the articles sent for examination, their particulars, from where and what circumstances they were seized should be mentioned. the serial number of the list and the label serial number must tally. - (vi) nature of examination must be mentioned for each item. - (vii) method of packing, name and number of the messenger constable should be mentioned. - (viii) samples seal - seals on the wooden box and inner packet must be similar. wax or lac, must be applied on the seals.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) police

page_content: any police officer, who is conducting investigation, may examine orally any witness, who is acquainted with the facts of the case. the examination of a witness and recording his statement by a police officer during the course of investigation dealt with the sections 160,161,162,163,154 and 171 of the cr.p.c. the competency and examination of a witness is dealt in sec. 118 to 134 of iea. the examination of witness in criminal case by a police officer is an important task which must be done diligently and intelligently.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) judicial magistrate:

page_content: in important cases judicial magistrate is competent to record statement under section 164 cr.p.c. if an accused person, on being arrested, expresses his willingness to make a confession, his confession may be recorded under section 164 of cr.p.c by the competent magistrate.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) complainant/victim:

page_content: complainant is a person who gives first information about a crime and gives the scope to the police to do justice. the information given by the complainant must be written in the words of complainant.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) accused:

page_content: accused may also be examined according to sec 161 of cr.p.c. it is important and necessary for an investigating officer to record the statement of an accused person in complicated cases. the investigating officer should secure the presence of two respectable and independent witnesses of the locality and in their presence record the confessional statement.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) eye witness:

page_content: a person who directly witnesses the incident is called eye witness and his statement should record under section 161 cr.p.c.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) other witness:

page_content: the statement of each witness should be recorded separately, in direct form and as far as possible in his own language and with the full version of what he states.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) at the scene of offence:

page_content: all-important witnesses should be questioned at the scene of offence because their memory reacts better that way.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) in the police station

page_content: the investigation officer may by an order in writing, require the attendance before himself of any person who appears to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case and such person shall attend as so required. the io should arrange to be made for his return to the dwelling place and he may not be made to spend his money to reach home.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) at the residence:

page_content: the investigation officer is not vested with powers to require the attendance of a woman of any age and a male who is below 15 yrs of age or elderly persons before him for examining her/him as witness. he shall go to the place of residence of or choice of the victim or witness of such woman or male and examine and record the statement there itself.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) in the court:

page_content: in some cases witnesses may be produced before judicial magistrate competent to record such statements under sec-164 cr.p.c. in some cases accused person on being arrested expresses his willingness to make a confession. his confession may be got recorded u/s 164 cr.p.c. by the competent magistrate.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (e) in the hospital:

page_content: complainant comes to the police station with injuries, he should be sent to hospital immediately and statement can be recorded in the hospital.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (f) at the jail:

page_content: any police officer not below the rank of an inspector may be permitted enter the jail to interview the prisoners on a letter of authority to the superintendent of jails from sp as per the prisoners act.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: confession: iea section 24

page_content: a confession made by an accused is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise, having reference to the charge against the accused person.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 25 iea

page_content: no confession made to a police officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 26 iea

page_content: no confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police officer, unless it is made in the immediate presence of a magistrate shall be proved as against such person.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: confession leading to recovery- section 27 iea

page_content: the statement is given by an accused to a police officer while in custody which reveals the discovery of any material fact and in consequence of that statement if that material fact is discovered, that statement is admissible to the extent of such discovery.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: recording of statements of witnesses & confessions of accused by a magistrate

page_content: s.o. 431-1. in important cases, witnesses may be produced before judicial magistrate competent to record such statements under section 164 cr.p.c. such statements can be used to contradict the statements given by the same person during the course of enquiry or trial. if he retracts later he can be prosecuted for giving false evidence.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: confession statement given to police o fficer under pota act-2002:

page_content: under the telangana control of organized crime act 2001 and prevention of terrorism act (pota) 2002, confessional statements made to police officer not below the rank of the superintendent of police and recorded by such police officer either in writing or on any mechanical devices can be admissible in the trial of such person or co-accused, abettor or conspirator provided that, the co-accused, abettor or conspirator is charged and tried in the same case together with the accused. the confession should be recorded in a free atmosphere in the same language in which the person is examined and as narrated by him.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 27 of iea act:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 27 of iea act:

page_content: 1. as soon as it appears to the investigating officer that an accused is likely to make a confession leading to the discovery of a fact, the investigating officer should secure the presence of two respectable and independent witnesses of the locality and in their presence record the confessional statement, obtaining the signatures of the witnesses on the record. 2. the statement of the accused should be in the first person and in the very words of the accused. 3. when a discovery is made as the result of the statement of the accused at another place, a separate panchanama should be drawn up for the discovery. 4. giving information and recovery that follows it are two different transactions and a separate panchanama should be recorded for each of 5. them. 5. the information given by an accused person should not be mixed up in the panchanama drawn up for the recovery made in consequence 7. of such information. 6. but if other accused persons suspected to have taken part in burying the property at the place also give the same information subsequently the discovery cannot be attributed to second person. 7. these confessional statements cannot be said to have led to the discovery of the property which has already been discovered and are not, therefore, relevant under that section. 8. the accused must be in custody of police for a confession to come under purview of section 27 of evidence act. 9. even a man released on bail and giving information leading to discovery of a fact can be deemed to be in police custody within meaning of section 27. 10. arrest and custody are not synonymous. a person can be in custody without being formally arrested.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: extra judicial confession:

page_content: extra judicial confession is one which is made outside the court of judicial proceedings. ex; a confession made to a private person. a confession made to a magistrate immediately after commission of an offence and before registration of fir.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: dying declaration:

page_content: dying declaration is evidence given by a person before his death. it has got very important evidentiary value in the court of law. section 32 (1) of the i.e.a explains about the dying declaration. normally if a person is admitted in the hospital with severe injuries and is on death bed, doctor will send intimation to police to record his statement as well as intimation to the judicial magistrate to record his dying declaration.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: dying declaration:

page_content: after receipt of the intimation, i.o goes to the hospital, records the statement of the injured and if necessary, he also sends requisition to the magistrate to record the dying declaration. in emergency conditions, anybody including doctor and police can record dying declaration, but the d.d record by the judicial magistrate has more evidentiary value over the d.d. recorded by others. if the court believes the d.d, it can convict the accused based only on its evidentiary value. but, in normal circumstances d.d requires corroborative evidence or circumstantial evidence to prove the guilt of the accused.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: dying declaration:

page_content: while recording the d.d of a person, he should be in full senses and depose evidence voluntarily and not under the influence of others. hence, it is better to obtain a certificate from the attending doctor on the d.d itself, stating that the injured is in conscious state and is in a position to depose evidence. during the trial of the case the judicial magistrate may be examined as witness.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) criminal law (amendment) act, 2013:

page_content: the statement of a woman against whom an offence under section 354, a,b,c,d, section 376, a,ab,b,c,d,da,db,e, or section 509 of the indian penal code is alleged to have been committed or attempted, shall be recorded, by a woman police officer or any woman officer.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) criminal law (amendment) act, 2013:

page_content: they should be examined at their residence and preferable in the company of another woman (women pc). they cannot be summoned to the police station for recording of statements. the statement of the child shall be recorded at the residence of the child or at a place where he usually resides or at the place of his choice and as far as practicable by a woman police officer not below the rank of sub-inspector. the police officer while recording the statement of the child shall not be in uniform.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) criminal law (amendment) act, 2013:

page_content: the judicial magistrate shall record the statement of the person against whom such offence has been committed in the manner prescribed in subsection (5), as soon as the commission of the offence is brought to the notice of the police.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) precautions while recording - female witness:

page_content: the women victim should be dealt with utmost sympathy and sensitivity. behavior towards the victim should be extremely courteous. no embarrassing or indecent questions should be put to the victim.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) precautions while recording - child witness:

page_content: a child is competent witness u/s 118 of the evidence act. child witness should be handled with care and affection. leading questions should be avoided. the advantage of child witness is that their observation is keen and truth is only likely to come as they are innocent and are not carried by ill-conceived notions.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) precautions while recording - child witness:

page_content: the statement of the child shall be recorded at the residence of the child at a place where he usually resides.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) precautions while recording - child witness:

page_content: the police officer making the investigation, shall, while examining the child, ensure that at no point of time the child comes in the contact in any way with the accused. the io may take the assistance of a translator or an interpreter, having such qualifications, experience and on payment of such fees as may be prescribed, while recording the statement of the child.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) re-examination of witnesses by police:

page_content: when a case is transferred from one investigating officer should another, the officer taking over the investigation shall take charge of the diary, noting therein the date of assuming charge of the investigation of the case.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (d) re-examination of witnesses by police:

page_content: succeeding investigating officer to re-examine all the important witnesses already examined, to ascertain the facts and the circumstances of the case. if any additional facts are spoken to by any witness, the second io will have to record those facts and note such other facts as may be necessary in clarification of the facts and omissions in the statement already recorded.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (e) audio video recording of the statements

page_content: the magistrate or the police officer, as the case may be, may, in the case of a child having a mental or physical disability, seek the assistance of a special educator or any person familiar with the manner of communication of the child or an expert in that field, having such qualifications, experience and on payment of such fees as may be prescribed, to record the statement of the child. wherever possible, the magistrate or the police officer, as the case may be, shall ensure that the statement of the child is also recorded by audio-video electronic means. further in some important cases like pocso act and sc & st poa act the statements of the victim and witnesses are to be recorded under videography.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (e) audio video recording of the statements

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: documentary evidence

page_content: the direct evidence, which is documentary in nature, may be either primary evidence or secondary evidence. primary evidence may be proved directly in the court by filing the original document itself. if the original is lost, the carbon or xerox copy of the document intended to be proved may be filed and it is called as secondary evidence. documents play an important role in investigation. roughly 70% of physical evidence relates to disputed documents

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: documentary evidence

page_content: therefore the io should understand the various types of questioned documents, the problems of documents examination, the care and preservation of documents and the assistance he can obtain from document experts.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: kinds of documents / writings:

page_content: in general there are three kinds of documents / writings. they are admitted, questioned and specimen documents/ writings. when the issue is one of establishing the identity of the writer of a document or the genuineness of it, the expert will need to compare it with other documents of known origin. documentary evidence is very much important to prove the offence against the accused.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: admitted document/writings:

page_content: admitted writings are those written by a person in his/her routine course of activity/business, which are freely written, natural. such writings are normally available in case of employees, students, working class people, professionals, business men etc. the admitted standard material can be procured in the following forms of documents.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: admitted document/writings:

page_content: - (i) personal correspondence: post cards, applications for employment/loans/diaries/account books etc. - (ii) academic records: school/college/applications, answer scripts, notes, records, project reports etc. - (iii) financial and business documents: passed bank cheques, applications for demand drafts, withdrawal forms, account opening forms, specimen signature cards, ledgers, receipts etc. - (iv) vocational & professional documents: official correspondence, leave letters, applications for loans, attendance register, legal documents, etc.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: admitted document/writings:

page_content: the most suitable admitted standards are those which belong to the same general class (post-card with post-card) and contemporary (near about date/month/year) to the questioned documents.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: questioned document / writings:

page_content: any object that contains handwritten, typewritten or printer marking whose source or authenticity is in doubt is referred to as questioned document. all questioned documents involved in a particular investigation should be submitted to the forensic science laboratory for examination. questioned documents are identified by comparison of similarities.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: sample document / writings: (specimen)

page_content: sample writings a especially written by the suspect for the purpose of comparison with the disputed documents. this is usually done either in the open court or in the presence of presiding officer. for collection of such specimens, there are some essential points to be followed to facilitate the experts' examination meaningful, objective and conclusive.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: sample document / writings: (specimen)

page_content: - (i) the suspect should be seated in a comfortable posture to ensure comfortable and natural writing conditions. - (ii) he/she should be supplied similar paper and writing instruments (pen/ball pen) as that of the questioned document. - (iii) the suspect should not be shown the questioned document - (iv) dictation should be given to the suspect at different intervals to prevent the suspect from imitating the same style. - (v) after each dictation, the sheet of specimens should be removed, and a fresh sheet of paper to be supplied. - (vi) the suspect should be encouraged to write the bits and pieces in the questioned sample several times. - (vii) at least 3 specimens should be taken. in case of deliberate efforts to disguise, more specimens should be taken. - (viii) each and every specimen sheet should be attested by the investigating officer along with the signatures of the witness with date.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: forged documents:

page_content: forgery is an attempt made by a person to defraud another by falsely making altering a signature or any part of document. forgery is the process of making, adapting, or imitating objects, statistics, or documents with the intent to deceive for the sake of altering the public perception, or to earn profit by selling the forged item.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: suicide notes:

page_content: the letter written by the person just before committing suicide is called suicide note. the investigating officer has to search for the suicide note at the scene. suicide notes are generally found in note books, pooja room, diaries and on plain papers. suicide notes are helpful in establishing the motive behind the suicide-if any person is responsible for the death, to know the state of mind of the person just before committing suicide. sometimes a fake suicide note may be planted to show a homicide as a suicide. care should be taken to check the hand writing of the deceased. the suicide note should be sent to fsl for handwriting analysis along with the old handwritings like notebooks and official correspondence, investigating officer should search the scene thoroughly for suicide notes.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: directions to produce documentary evidence - sec 91 cr.p.c:

page_content: whenever any officer in charge of police station or investigation officer considers that the production of any document or other thing is necessary for the purpose of investigation he may issue a written requisition to the person in whose possession or power such documents is believed to be requiring him to attend and produce it at a time and place stated in the order. document should be seized after conducting a search of a place where such document is kept.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: articles/documents in custody of postal department - sec 92 cr.p.c:

page_content: the instructions contained in the posts and telegraph manual, 1950 regarding production of records in the custody of the post office are given below for information of police officers making investigation of cases involving such records. records of a post office or mail office should be produced, and information available in them should be given on the written order of any police officer who is making an investigation under the cr.p.c., but only those entries in the records which relate to the person or persons charged with the offence under investigation or which are relevant to that offence should be disclosed. in any other case, the official in charge of the office should, without delay, refer for orders to the head of the circle, who will decide whether or not, under section 12 of the indian evidence act (1 of 1987), the information asked for should be withheld.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: articles/documents in custody of postal department - sec 92 cr.p.c:

page_content: when the information asked for by a police is not available in the records of the office concerned, the police or the excise officer should be informed accordingly, irrespective of the question whether the information, if available, might or might not be given'. if any document, parcel or thing in the custody of a postal or telegraph authority is required for the purpose of any investigation, the district magistrate, chief judicial magistrate, court of sessions or high court may require the postal or telegraph authority as the case may be to deliver the document to such person (s. 92 cr.p.c).

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: articles/documents in custody of postal department - sec 92 cr.p.c:

page_content: investigating police officers will be given all facilities to inspect within the respective offices the original documents in the custody of postal offices. they will also have facilities to take out copies of such documents (including photostat copies). even where an original document has to be shown to a witness during the process of investigation, it will be possible in many cases to have that carried out at the postal office.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: articles/documents in custody of postal department - sec 92 cr.p.c:

page_content: the above instructions apply to documents, in the possession of the posts and telegraphs department. police officers are strictly forbidden to resort to the use of sections 91 and 92 of the code of criminal procedure to secure original documents from postal offices.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: how photographs should be taken

page_content: - before commencing the work, the whole area should be inspected and all the shots required should be carefully planned. - while planning the number of shots, ensure that enough overlapping shots are included to sequentialise the scene. - initially sufficient number of shots should be taken to give an overall perspective of the scene of crime, approach to the place, entry, access and exit points. - while photographing evidentiary items, indoors or outdoors, enough close-up shots should be taken to bring out interrelationships and to capture minute details.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: how photographs should be taken

page_content: - overall view, - mid-range view, and - close-up view of all important items of evidence - the chance fingerprints, footprints, etc. left at the scene of crime, should always be exposed from a close distance. - a scale or foot rule should be placed beside evidentiary items such as footprints, knife, injuries, cut marks, etc. to indicate the actual size in the enlarged photograph. - it is worthwhile to include separate numbered cards or plates for each evidentiary item so as to ensure proper identification at a later stage.

metadata.heading: the observation notes should cover the following points: -

page_content: - technical information such as type of camera, focal length of lens, speed of the lens, effective aperture, shutter speed, film speed, type of flash. similar information should also be maintained for digital cameras. - special techniques such as oblique light, flood lamps, flashlight, uv light, poly light, filters etc., if used, should be noted against each shot. - light and weather conditions in cases of outdoor shots.

page_content: - (a) the process of video documentation is similar to photography. - (b) in a burglary case for example the videography should start at the - (c) actual/suspected point of entry, - (d) cover the crime scene, - (e) point of exit and line of retreat. - (f) all-important spots can be focused through a zoom lens and more time can be allotted to cover from all angles - (g) all the precautions suggested earlier for photography should also be followed for these recordings.

page_content: - the principle of exchange propounded by french scientist professor edmund locard in 1928, which says' whenever any two objects or materials, animate or inanimate, come into contact with each other, - there is always transference of material from each object on to the other'. - it is therefore the primary responsibility of investigating officers to search, identify and collect such evidence independently or with the assistance of 'clues' team.

metadata.heading: lifting of finger prints, foot prints, portrait parley

page_content: - always take care of the scene of crime articles which are exposed bearing chance prints by providing shelter or cover to protect them from sun, rain, dust etc., to prevent damage to the clues, before they are examined by an fp expert. - always make it a point to utilize the services of a fingerprint expert if any chance prints are suspected to exist on any article at the scene of crime.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: lifting of latent finger prints:

page_content: - when chance prints are found in narrow nooks and corners, or the inner surfaces of jewel cases, jars and flower vases, it is not possible to photograph them. - the finger prints should be secured and preserved by the process of lifting. - the method adopted in shifting fps is to press the gummy surface of a transparent cellophane tape against the developed chance prints without forming air bubbles lift it and transfer them to a contrast lift cards. - lifting should be resorted to only for those prints where photography is not possible, as there is every risk of the prints getting partly or wholly damaged in the process. - lifting of chance prints also can be done on all surfaces after photography,

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: lifting of latent finger prints:

page_content: - making use of latest methods like iodine fuming, ninhydrin, glueing and applying multiple wave length light derived from polilight, poliray etc. or laser beams. - remember always to use separate containers for preserving severed fingers of an unidentified dead body.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: the methods commonly used for collecting footprints are:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: by preserving the original impression intact: if the foot print is found on a portable object such as a mat, slab, plank, card board, etc. it is convenient to carry the object bearing the print to the laboratory for photography and further analysis

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: - it is advisable to take the photograph of the print even in such cases before removing them as there is always the risk of the original impression being damaged in packing or transit. - at least three photographs are to be taken, one direct view from the top and the other two from either side at an angle of 45 . - before taking the photograph place an identification slip or card (containing crime number, section of law, date, police station, district etc. with initials of the io) close to one side of the print and a foot rule on the other side to record the actual size of the footprint.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: tracing: tracing is a rough method to record surface prints. a glass (or preferably a celluloid sheet) is held over the print as close as possible. care should be taken to ensure that it does not come in contact with the print. then a sketch pen draws the contours along the print line. details such as the characters of the toes, ball of the foot, instep and heel together with crease; cracks, warts, etc. should carefully be traced on the sheet.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: - after the tracing is complete the sheet is removed and all the particulars of the case should be written on the glass together with the signature of the investigating officer and the witnesses. - on the basis of such prints on glass any number of tracings can be obtained on tracing paper. - the glass sheet and a tracing paper should be packed in between two cardboards for transport to the court.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: lifting: when the print is formed on a surface that is of a similar colour to the print, for instance, a blood stained print formed on a red surface, dusty (white) print formed on a white surface, dark (black) print formed on a black surface,

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: - it becomes difficult to photograph the print because of the similar colour of the background. - footprints can also be lifted using photographic film or transparent adhesive tape. the fixed photographic film is moistened in water for few minutes until the emulsion side becomes tacky and then placed over the print with the sensitive side in contact with the print.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: a uniform pressure is applied over the film for few seconds and it is then lifted carefully. the print is transferred on to the film, which can then be used as a negative to make copies from.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: collection of footprints:

page_content: casting: casts are prepared from foot impressions (sunken foot prints), tyre impressions or other impressions left behind by the criminal. a variety of materials are used to prepare the cast, e.g., plaster of paris, wax, resins, plastic powders, modelling clay, sulphur, lead, wood metal, plasticine, moulage material, silicone, etc.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: portrait parley:

page_content: portrait parle is french term referring to a picture of a suspect in both front and profile views. modernization has led this to be our 'mug shot' of a suspect. we still use front and profile views but we have added in written descriptions of the suspect as well. we use portrait parles for finding suspects and missing persons because of the thoroughness of the descriptions.

metadata.heading: portrait parley:

page_content: key descriptions for portrait parleys are as follows. notice that the descriptions essentially profile a person's whole personality, not just a physical descriptor.

metadata.heading: criminal justice applications

page_content: just like automated fingerprint identification, facial recognition helps police to manage the records of people of interest. among other tasks, it provides an alternate, or additional, method to make sure that databases do not contain multiple records for a single individual. while this task is routinely performed by police agencies using fingerprints, facial recognition provides benefits when fingerprint data either does not exist or is not easily shared between agencies:

metadata.heading: criminal justice applications

page_content: the software available with police allows people to be identified, current automated face extraction techniques work well with almost full-frontal views of faces. the various parts of face/body of different types are shown in computer software to the victims of offence to identify the accused. some body parts are given below the facial features and if required figure of accused are drawn basing on the version of victim which is helpful to trace the accused.

page_content: the part of earth's surface that is not covered by water.

metadata.heading: types of land

page_content: - dry/wet land - agriculture/non-agriculture land - government land - private land - patta land - inam land - wakf board land, - temple lands (endowment land) - bhoodan land - forest land - ceiling and surplus land - urban/built up land

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: what is a land dispute?

page_content: land dispute involves conflicting claims to rights in land by two or more parties, focused on a particular piece of land, which can be addressed within the existing legal frameworks. land dispute may, or may not reflect some border conflict over land. however, we cannot define a land dispute in a single statement. each scenario is different.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (a) trespass

page_content: - (1) a person enters or encroaches upon property which is in the possession of others, with the intent to commit an offence, intimidate, insult, or annoy any person in possession of such property. - (2) a person entering the property and dispossessing those who are already in possession of such property. - (3) a person encroaches upon the neighboring land, by damaging/ removing the boundaries. - (4) a person encroaches upon the land belonging to others and takes up construction activity. - (5) a person trespasses into other's property and damages the existing boundaries like fencing, boundary stones and wall and threatens the persons who are safeguarding (watchman, security guards, tenants) in the absence of the landlords.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: land disputes

page_content: - (6) a person trespasses into other's land and damages its nature, or demolishes the existing structures. - (7) a person enters other's property and hurts, threatens, and annoys a person who is already in possession of that land/property. - (8) a person, or a group, does unlawful activity of grabbing government land, a religious of charitable institution or endowment, including wakf or any other land, either by force, threat, or inducement with the criminal intent to take unauthorised possession of such property. the complainant should be a competent authority, i.e, a member of revenue, wakf board, forest department, endowment, department or any other government/ quasi-government agency. - (9) encroaching upon the ftl (full tank level) land, with criminal intent.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf

page_content: creation of documents, where there is no land physically.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (b) fake/false documents:

page_content: - (1) related to the revenue department : title deeds, pasbooks, land/ house allotment certificate, mutation certificates, ror, pahanis, survey report/panchanamas, land conversion certificates, and ordrs of the rd, joint collector, and district collector. - (2) related to panchayat raj department: gp layouts, gp resolutions, approval of layouts by the panchayat raj officer, and reports of the panchyat secretary. - (3) related to the ghmc/hmda : property tax, building permission, layout, water tap connection bills, voter id card, ration card, aadhaar card, assessment, occupancy certificate, building penalizaion scheme final order, property valuation reports, mortgage and mortgage release orders, death certificates, lrs (land regulatizaion scheme). - (4) related to the office of the sub registrar : impersonation (instead of original owners, a fake/fictitious person executes the documents, personating himself as the original owner), validation to time expired unregistered document, rectification documents, false registered sale deed, false registered gpa, creating a false document taking advantage of missing volumes/records in sro office. - (5) related to the judicial court: court decrees, perpetual injunctions, interim injunctions, stay orders, judgments, status quos, and order of cancellation of registered documents. - (6) related to the electricity department: service connection documents, electricity bills. - (7) related to irrigation department: non ftl (full tank level) land certificate, flood embankment, drainage design, kuntas, reservoirs, tanks, anicuts, canals, their distributaries.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: (c) cheating (with criminal intent and knowledge)

page_content: - (1) multiple sale deeds, after taking sale consideration - (2) executing agreements/ sale deeds, without any right over the property - (3) executing agreements/sale deeds showing excess land (intentionally) - (4) executing agreements/sale deeds in an unauthrised manner (no right over the property), without having a supporting link document - (5) selling the land which was already acquired (in the process of acquisition) by government agencies, after receiving the compensation - (6) physical possession of the land differs from the survey number on the document. - (7) selling open land earmarked for community amenities/social infrastructures after releasing the mortgages from hmda/ghmc. - (8) executing sale agreement/sale deed in respect of mortgaged plot/ flat with hmda/ghmc and local bodies (licensing authority) before the release of mortgage by the competent authority. - (9) pre-launching sale transactions, i.e, before getting permissions from competent authorities, not acquiring required lands, the vendors go for sale of properties in the market through wide publicity and disappear after swindling the money. - (10) obtaining multiple loans on the property from various banks. - (11) obtaining loans from banks by inflating the price of property with the help of property valuators. - (12) obtaining loans from banks, showing excess physical area, and - (13) unregistered sale deeds/agreement of sale with regards to lands related to inam, laoni and assigned categories. knowingly selling the mortgaged lands without obtaining noc.

page_content: note: if a complaint is lodged by any authorized person/competent authority of any government/public agency with regard to land/property, the police officer should invariably register a case and investigate the matter.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: when does a land/property dispute become a civil case?

page_content: - (1) the complainant is unable to reveal prima-facie, i.e forgery, dispossession, trespass, damage to property, fake document, impersonation, criminal intimidation, or physical assault - (2) on the strength of an injunction order, an individual seeks dispossession of persons who are already in possession of the property. - (3) on the strength of valid sale deed, an individual seeks dispossession of persons who are already in possession of the property. - (4) family land/property disputesclaiming land without valid inheritance partition deeds, family gift deeds, and genuine will deeds. - (5) civil matters pending in the court with regard to title declaration: appeals, for any specific directions. - (6) tenant defaulting on rental payments. - (7) tenant subleasing the property to others. - (8) tenant/landlord violates the lease agreements and - (9) boundary and passage disputes.

metadata.heading: how is ownership of immobile property acquired?

page_content: - (1) through will - (2) by way of purchase, etc. - (3) through gift, trust, settlement deeds - (4) thorough grant, sanad/inam by the government - (5) through partition deed, and - (6) through a court decree

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: what is the process for registering land?

page_content: - verbal contract: both the seller and the buyer can have a verbal agreement over the purchase or sale, of the land. - preparation of contract: once the contract is verbally agreed upon, it needs to be recorded on paper. this is done by preparing the agreement and sale document. - pay stamp duty: once the deed is made, you are required to pay the stamp duty; the stamp duty value may range from 3-7 percent. - execute and register sale deed: the next step is to execute the sale deed and register the same by visiting the sub-registrar's office. - property mutations: the final step is getting the mutation of the property done in the land and revenue records. this can be done by visiting the ghmc/ municipal authorities.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: role of police officers in land related civil/criminal cases the role of a police officer while dealing with land related enquiries and investigation:

page_content: the police officer should have

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: role of police officers in land related civil/criminal cases the role of a police officer while dealing with land related enquiries and investigation:

page_content: - a thorough knowledge of the related acts/rules. - ability to understand the core issue/subject matter. - procedural fluency. - ability to critically scrutinize the available documents. - patience to listen to the claims of either party. - conceptual understating (connecting the links) - the ability to be judicious, impartial, and neutral - independent thinking and consciousness. - willingness to take legal advice. - the talent for taking actions within the ambit of law (police protection or aid) - the ability to be uninfluenced (politically or bureaucratically) - the discretion of not misusing one's official position for pecuniary benefits. - the fair-mindedness to not evict physical possession, and - the ability for collecting relevant evidence (oral/documentary) for successful disposal of the case.
