metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: key points to note to investigate/enquire land/property disputes

page_content: - (1) land address: i.e survey number, extent, boundaries, mandal. in a number of in stances, the survey number of the landowners differs from what is specified in the records. records are available, but physical land is not there. in order to establish the exact address of the land, verify the settlement/setwar document, khasra pahani and subsequent pahanis and their extent, visit the field and find out the survey number. - (2) who is owner of the land? the right way is to look at ror or ib, not the pass book, because a passbook is the extract for ror/dharani (sethwar, khasra or seshala). then establish the link between ror and setwar. ec and pahanies will help in finding out owner. if all are matching, the title is strong, if not, wrong. - (3) classification of land: i.e government land, forest land, ceiling, bhoodhan, assignment. these details are available in pahani, but there is every chance of mistakes in it. see the setwar for classification of land, because the setwar prevails over the pahani. the authority to change the classification vests with the joint collector. classification can be changed in the pahani through his order. - (4) who is in possession of the land? the cultivator column in the pahani clarifies it, but the present pahanis are not having the cultivation column (after dharani implementation). now the owner is presumed to be possessor of the land. physical verification will resolve it. - (5) find out what is the land problem: identity the nature of the land or property dispute. - (6) which act/law is applicable for the problem? - (7) which is the appropriate forum to address the issue?

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: keys points for police to observe while dealing land disputes:

page_content: 1. police never evict the possessor. whoever is in factual/physical possession, it is the bounden duty of the police to see that the possession should not be disturbed. 2. police can intervene when there could be a breach of the peace by initiating 145, 107 crpc proceeding by sending a report to the executive magistrate. 3. comply with the court orders for police protection or aid.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: keys points for police to observe while dealing land disputes:

page_content: can be the absolute owners of the property

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: keys points for police to observe while dealing land disputes:

page_content: 4. when there is no specific direction from the court, the police should not interfere. 5. police should ensure law order by providing police protection to the advocate commissioner or any authorized person deputed by the court. 6. the police have no rights to declare the title/ownership of the property. 7. any illegal occupants should be evicted through court orders only. 8. protection to be given at the time of survey to the concerned authorities on their written request. 9. no protection should be given for private survey. 10. even if a civil dispute is pending in any court, a criminal wrong may be investigated and the case should be finalized on its merits, but not to close as 'civil nature' without investigation.

metadata.heading: simulated case:

page_content: for this practical (simulation) exercise a mock case file is developed. the details of present case are a piece of land measuring 500 sq.yds. in survey no. 100 in rajendranager (v) allotted by the govt. to freedom fighter nadipalli narayan rao s/o krishna rao, age 70 years r/o h-no. 1-2-145 rajendranager (v) long back, but the land borders were not clearly demarcated while handing over allotment document to him. due to old age the said freedom fighter did not pursue the matter with the concerned authorities about the demarcation of plot. a local farmer akula srinivas s/o ramaiah age 45 years h. no. 1-3-150, rajendranager (v) who is having land adjacent to the land allotted to freedom fighter nadipalli narayan rao, has been claiming the part of said land and encroaching in to the land by ploughing the land and creating trouble to freedom fighter.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 151 in the code of criminal procedure, 1973

page_content: 1. a police officer knowing of a design to commit any cognizable offence may arrest, without orders from a magistrate and without a warrant, the person so designing, if it appears to such officer that the commission of the offence cannot be otherwise prevented.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 145 (1) in the code of criminal procedure, 1973

page_content: whenever an executive magistrate is satisfied from a report of a police officer or upon other information that a dispute likely to cause a breach of the peace exists concerning any land or water or the boundaries thereof, within his local jurisdiction, he shall make an order in writing, stating the grounds of his being so satisfied, and requiring the parties concerned in such dispute to attend his court in person or by pleader, on a specified date and time, and to put in written statements of their respective claims as respects the fact of actual possession of the subject of dispute.

metadata.heading: section 100 cr.p.c:

page_content: 1. the officer can serve notice on the witness for the above purpose. 2. the following shall be observed during the search:- 3. (a) the search shall be made in the presence of witnesses. 4. (b) list of articles seized and the places where they are found is to be prepared. 5. (c) the list is to be prepared by the officer and signed by witnesses. 6. (d) the persons witnessing the search shall not be summoned to attend the court as witnesses for the search unless specifically summoned by it. 2. the occupant or owner shall be allowed to be present and permitted to attend. a copy of the list prepared of the articles seized must be delivered to him. 3. whenever a search is made of a person and any article seized from searched person, a list of articles seized must be delivered to him. 4. a person when called for refuses or neglects to attend and witness a search under section his liable under section 187 cr.p.c.

page_content: whenever a person is arrested by a police officer making the arrest may search such person and place in safe custody all articles, other than his clothes. any article seized from the person shall be listed and the list shall be furnished to the arrested person.

page_content: whenever a female is to be searched, the search shall be made by another female with strict regard to decency. if any incriminating material

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: search with warrant: sections 93, 94,95, 97 and 100 cr.p.c: search for documents (section 93 cr.p.c)

page_content: 1. any magistrate can issue a warrant except for items in the post and telegrams. a magistrate may issue a warrant under this section even if there is no proceeding pending in his court. 2. a warrant may be issued when there is reason to believe that the articles or documents cannot be produced by a summons. it can be issued when it is not certain that these are with the particular person. or when the court considers that the enquiry, trial or other proceedings will be served by a general search or inspection. 3. the warrant can be issued to any person including the police officer. 4. a warrant can be in general terms or may be restricted to a particular 5. place of search. 5. for the search of a document, parcel or article in the custody of postal or telegraphs authority only the district magistrate or the chief judicial magistrate is competent to issue a warrant.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: search for stolen property (section 94 cr.p.c)

page_content: 1. whenever a magistrate has reason to believe that a place is used for 2. the deposit or sale of stolen property or objectionable article, he may issue a warrant to enter, to search, to take possession of such an article, to convey such property before a magistrate or guard it, to take into custody every person suspected to be involved. objectionable articles in this section include counterfeit coins, counterfeit currency, forged document, false seals, and obscene objects. 2. instruments used for production of the above.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: search of publications (section 95 cr.p.c)

page_content: 1. the warrant may be issued to the police officer not below the rank of s.i. to enter any premises and search. 2. the warrant may be issued for the seizure of any news paper, book or document. a warrant may be issued if the state government declares by notifications specifying the reasons that the matter is punishable under sections 124 (a), 153 (a), 153 (b), 292, 293 and 295 (a) ipc and opines that the documents or book or news papers should be forfeited. documents include any painting, drawing or photograph or other visible representation. in case of emergency, the police officer can invoke section 165 cr.p.c and make searches 3. and seizures without obtaining a warrant from the magistrate.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 97 cr.p.c)

page_content: if any district magistrate, sub-divisional magistrate or magistrate of the first class has reason to believe that any person is confined under such circumstances that the confinement amounts to an offence, he may issue a search-warrant, and the person to whom such warrant is directed may search for the person so confined; and such search shall be made in accordance therewith, and the person, if found, shall be immediately taken before a magistrate, who shall make such order as in the circumstances of the case seems proper.

page_content: search memo and search list: a list of all articles shall be prepared and a copy furnished to the owner of the house. the following are the contents of the search list.

page_content: 1. place 2. date and time 3. under which order search is made 4. details of the officer who conducted the search 5. list of articles found 6. signature of the person / owner / resident 7. signatures of the witnesses 8. signature of the io / officer

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: procedure of seizure:

page_content: seizure means the act of taking the physical possession of property. a search is made for seizure. they are closely connected. search is made by a police officer to prove the occurrence of a crime. seizure not only helps in the detection of the case but is useful as evidence including the charge sheet against the accused. seizure is made

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: procedure of seizure:

page_content: - at the scene of crime - from witnesses - in search of various places of accused and - on the confession made by the accused, from his possession or at his instance. - every seizure is made in the presence of witnesses and record (panchanama) is made. - according to section 47 cr.p.c, if the accused has hidden in some place it can be searched and accused shall be arrested. - according to section 51 cr.p.c, whatever is found on the accused except his clothing shall be seized. a list of articles seized shall be prepared and receipt given to him. - u/s 52 cr.p.c the officer can seize any offensive weapons found on the arrested person. - any police officer may seize any property which may be suspected to be stolen u/s 102 cr.p.c.

page_content: procedure for disposal of perishable property/drugs:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: drafting of seizure proceedings:

page_content: 1. when ever any perishable articles are found during the search, they have to be seized under the cover of panchanama, in the presence of independent witnesses. the details are to be given to the executive magistrate/judicial magistrate who in turn disposes them after conducting an auction. the proceedings of the auction are to be submitted to the court having jurisdiction of the area. in case, drugs are found they have to be seized under the cover of panchanama in the presence of independent witnesses and information sent to the controller of drugs and the district collector to take necessary action and disposal.

metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: importance of case diary - part-i:

page_content: case diaries are maintained as per the provisions of section 172 cr.p.c. every police officer making an investigation under this chapter shall day by day enter his proceedings of the investigation in a diary, setting forth the time at which the information reached him, the time at which he began and closed his investigation, the place or places visited by him, and a statement of the circumstances ascertained through his investigation.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: importance of case diary - part-i:

page_content: case diaries are the records of the entire investigation conducted by the investigating officers and different types of evidence gathered.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: importance of case diary - part-i:

page_content: it will be helpful to record the entire course of investigation. even if the investigation officer is transferred the case diary helps in the continuation of investigation.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: importance of case diary - part-i:

page_content: the case diary includes

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: importance of case diary - part-i:

page_content: - the 161 statements of the witnesses - the copy of complaint - fir copy - rough sketch of the scene of offence - the material object seized - the letter of advice to fsl for analysis - description of wounds - requisition sent to the medical officer - wound certificate - post mortem examination certificate - and all relevant records useful for investigation.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: safety of case diary part-i:

page_content: - the investigating officer and his superiors shall ensure the physical safety of the case diary as well as its contents. - the access to the case diaries is limited only to the io, the superior officers and the concerned legal officers who are in charge at a given time and not to others. - the officer who has custody of case diaries either as an io or a superior officer supervising investigation should ensure that it is handed over to their successors in office.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: points to be observed in writing case part-i (s.o. 477)

page_content: - the first case diary should commence with a brief summary of the fir, the time of receipt of the complaint, delay if any, in starting

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: points to be observed in writing case part-i (s.o. 477)

page_content: - for the scene, the time of departure and arrival at the scene, and description and plan of the scene as prescribed in order 420 and 421 of chapter 22. - in case where there is no scene of offence like in financial crimes, the records and places where such records or data recording, storage or retrieval systems are located, the method of crime as reflected at such places, objects, computers, documents etc. and the plan or sketch or diagram representing the crime scene may be described or drawn up as is possible. - a statement of circumstances ascertained through the investigation at the place or places visited by the investigating officer, and the date and hour of closing the investigation shall be noted. - every step taken by the io shall be mentioned as concisely as possible. - every clue obtained, even if at the time appears likely to be of no value, houses searched with reasons for the search and the names of witnesses to the search are to be noted. - property recovered its description and place where it was found to be noted. - arrests, information obtained which is likely to prove of value, and methods adopted by the suspects/accused are among the things to be mentioned in the case diary. - the substance of the statements of witnesses shall find place in the case diary. - the io shall record in form 65 the statements of persons examined by him in detail separately and attach to the case diary of the day as part ii. - case diaries and statement of witnesses will be duplicated by carbon process, or by photocopiers. the original is retained in the station and the other sent to the sdpo concerned who shall deal with the case diaries in the manner prescribed - in the concluding diary, the investigating officer shall record a summary of the reasons which have guided his final decision in the case. if he considers that there is no case, his reasons will, of course, be more detailed and fuller than the one sent up for trial. - the names of informers need not be entered in the diary and no court can compel an investigating officer to disclose the name of an informer. - when a case is transferred from one investigating officer to another, the officer taking over the investigation shall take charge of the diary, noting therein the date of assuming charge of the investigation of the case. - those who, under the orders and directions of the investigating officer, assist him by making any enquiries do not thereby become

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: points to be observed in writing case part-i (s.o. 477)

page_content: - investigating officers under chapter 12 of cr.p.c. therefore, it is not necessary for them to write case diaries under section 172 of cr.p.c. - the result of any enquiries such officers make or action they take pursuant to the orders or instructions of the investigating officer will be communicated by the former to the latter by means of a special report. - head constables will also make necessary entries in their notebooks. the investigating officer shall incorporate the gist of such special reports in his case diary u/s 172 cr.p.c. - remands should be applied for along with case diary. sub- section (1) of section 167 of cr.p.c requires a copy of the case diary to be sent when remand is sought. - the investigating officer should, therefore, prepare an additional copy of the case diary, when he is aware that he will have to send a prisoner for remand. - the statements recorded under section 161 cr.p.c till then should also be enclosed to the case diary. - case diaries should also be written in cases, referred by a magistrate to the police for investigation under section 155, 156 or 202 of cr.p.c. - statements of witnesses examined by the police during investigation should be recorded in form 65 and should be attached to the case diary for the day.

metadata.heading: wound certificate:

page_content: general duty doctors in all major hospitals -govt. and private are often unclear regarding when to label a case as medico-legal.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: wound certificate:

page_content: what is an injury report....? an injury report (wound certificate, medicolegal certificate, medico-legal case [mlc]) is a document prepared by the doctor in all medico-legal cases after treating the victim / accused.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: consent for examination:

page_content: - (1) patient arrested by the police - (i) if the patient has been arrested and brought by the police, no consent need be taken.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: i dentification marks:

page_content: two identification marks must be taken.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: general condition of the patient must be mentioned like:

page_content: - o whether the patient is conscious or unconscious - o if conscious, whether anxious, tense, afraid, agitated, subdued. - o bleeding from nostrils, ears, mouth, other natural orifices (vagina in case of sexual assault, anus in the case of buggery). - o pulse rate, blood pressure, temperature, whether in a state of shock, paralyzed etc. - o if the police want a statement from the victim, the doctor must first certify that the patient is compos mentis.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: type of each injury:

page_content: all injuries, however insignificant they may appear, should be recorded. proper, adequate, and complete documentation is very necessary for all medico-legal work. even old injuries should be recorded. type of each injury (e.g. whether it is an abrasion, contusion, laceration, incised wound, stab, burn, scald, fracture, dislocation of tooth etc.) should be noted. presence of any foreign material on the body or within the wound e.g. broken off point of a knife, bullets, coal, dirt, dust, fibers, glass, grass, gravel, grease, hair, metal, mud, oil, paint etc.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: age of each injury:

page_content: age of each injury should be noted after noting gross changes in the wounds (e.g. color of a bruise, condition of scab in abrasions, infection etc.).

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: nature of each injury:

page_content: against each injury, its nature should be noted (e.g. simple, grievous or dangerous)

metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf

metadata.heading: by what weapon i nflicted:

page_content: blunt or sharp weapon etc.

metadata.heading: remarks:

page_content: this would include entries not covered elsewhere, e.g. condition of clothes (whether they are blood stained or stained by other body fluids like semen, saliva etc., torn, buttons missing, burnt etc., or not) etc.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: autopsy report:

page_content: in a case of death of a person the io has to conduct inquest over the dead body of the deceased before the mediators and blood relatives and to forward the dead body to govt hospital with escort and a requisition addressed to the duty medical officer, with request to conduct postmortem examination (autopsy), to know the exact cause of the death of that person. the io should enclose the copy of the inquest report to the requisition to acquaint the facts of the case to the doctor.

page_content: post mortem report no. ...................... ref. no. ........................ body identified by: (relative of deceased) date and hour of receipt of inquest paper and dead body: date and hour of starting autopsy: date and hour of concluding autopsy: body brought and identified by: p.c. ............................... no. ............................. i.o. ....................... p.c. ............................... no. ............................ p.s. .......................

page_content: 5. post-mortem changes present (extent of rigor mortis, post-mortem lividity and putrefactive signs; stained blood vessels, greenish discoloration, odour, softening of eye balls, exudation from nose and mouth, ova or flies, moving maggots, blebs over body, peeling of cuticle, loosening of hair, thorax and abdomen burst, separation of sutures of skull, eye changes, adipocere, mummification.) 6. external appearance: condition of limbs, eyes, ears, nostrils, mouth, anus, vagina, and urethra.

metadata.heading: head and neck

page_content: 1. skull, brain, meninges and cerebral vessels. (note presence of any abnormal smell) 2. orbital, nasal and aural cavities, (examine if special indication present). 3. mouth, tongue. 4. neck, larynx, thyroid and other neck structures.

metadata.heading: chest (thorax)

page_content: 1. ribs and chest wall 2. diaphragm 3. oesophagus 4. trachea and bronchi 5. pleural cavities 6. lungs 7. heart and pericardial sac 8. large blood vessels

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: abdomen

page_content: 1. abdominal wall 2. peritoneal cavity 3. stomach and content 4. small intestine 5. large intestine, vermiform appendix, mesentery and pancreas 6. liver, gall bladder, biliary passages 7. spleen 8. kidney, renal pelvis, ureters 9. pelvic wall 10. urinary bladder and urethra 11. genital organs

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: spinal column

page_content: 1. spinal column and spinal cord (the spinal cord need only be opened and the cord examined if special indications are present).

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: additional remarks

page_content: in january 2016 four constables were convicted for torturing khicchi and causing his death in police custody.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: additional remarks

page_content: in handing down guilty verdicts, judge sm bhosale noted, 'the word 'custody' implies guardianship and protective care. even when applied to indicate arrest or incarceration, it does not carry any sinister symptoms of violence during custody.' the four policemen were each sentenced to seven years in prison for culpable homicide not amounting to murder and for voluntarily causing hurt to extract a confession.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: additional remarks

page_content: a critical piece of evidence in this case was the postmortem report. khicchi's post-mortem report shows 56 injuries and final cause of death as 'head injury with multiple injuries.' according to the report, all contusions were caused by a hard and blunt object.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: additional remarks

page_content: when the police attributed those injuries to a mob attack, khicchi's family members urged the police commissioner to transfer the investigation to an independent agency. in october 2013, the crime branch of the state criminal investigation department took over the case. a month later, the crime branch filed charges and arrested four police officers on murder charges.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: additional remarks

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: additional remarks

page_content: copy of post-mortem report of aniket khicchi dt. october 28, 2013

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: additional remarks

page_content: in khicchi's case, the court noted several procedural violations at the time of arrest. the arrest record was drawn up at 2.40 pm on october 26, but the policemen who took the two suspects into custody did not register a first information report or make an entry into the police station diary as required by law until after 8 pm. until that time, khicchi and vani were illegally detained and interrogated inside the police station. the court also said that even though the accused police officers claimed that khicchi was assaulted by a mob and that they had rescued him from about 60 to 70 people, the policemen did not take him for the required medical check-up.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: additional remarks

page_content: eyewitness, vani's testimony played an important role in ensuring the conviction of the accused police officials.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: legal provisions: sec 54a of cr.p.c:

page_content: where a person is arrested on a charge of committing an offence and his identification by any other person or persons is considered necessary for the purpose of investigation of such offence, the court, having jurisdiction, may on the request of the officer in charge of a police station, direct the person so arrested to subject himself to identification by any person or persons in such manner as the court may deem fit.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: principles regarding i dentification procedure of person or property:

page_content: test identification parade is useful in taking the investigation in the right direction. ti parade is to be corroborated with the other evidence

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: principles regarding i dentification procedure of person or property:

page_content: (sec 157 iea). ti parade is conducted by judicial magistrate to identify the offender or lost property by the witness (sec.9 iea). ti parade is taken up only when the accused is in judicial custody. requisition has to be filed to chief judicial magistrate. the police officers make all arrangements and completely withdraw. the accused should be mingled with persons of similar height, weight, stature, age and status in proportion of minimum of 1:5 and maximum of 1:10. witnesses should be called in one by one to identify the accused.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: i dentification of property by the complainants:

page_content: the identification of property takes place in the court only. each item of the property shall be put up separately for the parade. it shall be mixed up with four or five similar objects. before identification of the property the witness shall be asked the description of his property. the proceedings of identification of the property shall be drawn by the presiding officer.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: i dentification of the suspected photographs, cctv footage by the witness - responsibility of cctvs:

page_content: witnesses may be shown photographs of the suspect mixed with several non suspects and asked to identify. later regular identification parade should also be held. photographs should not be shown before t.i parade to witness. publication of the suspect photos in media is counterproductive. cctv footage should be seized by the io with the help of cyber crime experts and should not be published in the media.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: i dentification of the recovered weapons:

page_content: this is similar to property identification. the recovered weapons shall be mixed with similar weapons and asked to be identified by the witness. witness can be the expert also (sec.45 iea).

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: power of police to make measurements:

page_content: under the identification of the prisoners act 1920, police can take the finger print, photograph and other measurements if a person is convicted of an offence punishable with one year or more or ordered to give security for good behavior under section 110 cr.p.c. refusal to give measurements will be punishable under section 186 ipc. identification of prisoners rules 1975 (rule 12 & 14) gives the classes of persons whose finger prints and photographs are taken. this information should be maintained in police station records, dcrb/scrb and ncrb.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: role of women police in investigation:

page_content: 1. arrest: police officer shall not touch the person of the woman for making her arrest unless the police officer is a female (sec.46 cr.p.c). arrest of woman should not be made after sun set and before sun rise. 2. search: a woman suspect can be searched only by another woman.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: role of women police in investigation:

page_content: 3. escorts: women under trials or convicts shall be escorted by women police constables only. 4. recording statements: according to 154 cr.p.c any offence against woman which comes under sec 326b, 354-a, 354-b, 354-c, 354-d, section 376-a,b,c,d,e,ab,ba,da,db and 509 ipc. the statement of the victim shall be recorded by woman police officer or in the absence, by any woman officer.

page_content: the brief facts of the case are that on 17.7.17, complainant's daughter kum. poonam, aged 16yrs left their house at about 7pm informing him that she is going to attend a birth day party of her friend monica. till late in the night also when there was no response from poonam's cell, complainant lodged a report with rajendranagar ps at about 00:30 hrs that his daughter may have been kidnapped by unknown accused and asi sri krishna who was on duty at ps rajendranagar registered this complaint as in cr. no......601...../17 u/s 365 ipc and took up investigation. asi informed sho and other superior officers. on 18.7.17 at about 0900hrs on receipt of information that an unknown dead body is lying in the outskirts of himayatsagar, on north/west side, visited the said place along with complainant sanjay kumar, his wife sunita and they identified the dead body of girl found in bushes to be that of poonam their daughter who is aged 16 and suspected that poonam has been raped and murdered. an observation of scene of offence panchnama was conducted in the presence of panchwitnesses:

page_content: on18.7.17 during the inquest, examined parents of deceased sanjay kumar, sunita and ram reddy a neighbouring land owner and all of them unanimously opined that the death of poonam was apparently due to rape and murder. a memo was filed before this court adding sections 376, 302, 201 ipc r/w sec 5 of pocso act. the dead body was subjected to pm exam with a team of doctors including a lady doctor. spot opinion by asst prof forensic medicine sri narayan reddy is given where it is mentioned that poonam was raped and murdered by stab injuries over the breast and abdomen.

metadata.heading: memo for obtaining transit remand

page_content: the necessity of obtaining transit warrant arises, when the accused of a particular case is arrested by io in the place which is situated at a long distance from the place where jurisdictional magistrate court is situated. once an accused is arrested by io, he has power to keep him in his custody for 24 hours as per 167 cr.p.c. then io has to produce the accused in the court of nearest magistrate from the place of arrest and file a requisition mentioning the facts to issue transit warrant to permit him to take accused to his jurisdiction area and to produce him in the area jurisdiction court.

page_content: the word 'bail' has not been defined in law. the meaning of the term 'bail' is to set free a person who is under arrest, by taking security for his appearance in court. in schedule-i cr.p.c, the offences cognizable, noncognizable; bailable and non-bailable have been mentioned. provisions as to bail and bonds are mentioned from section 436 to 450 cr.p.c.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: bails

page_content: article -21 of the constitution of india states that, 'no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to the procedure established by law.'

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: bailable offence (section 436 cr.p.c)

page_content: it is the right of the arrested/detained person in bailable offences to obtain bail. either the accused may be enlarged on bail by the sho or court on executing a personal bond. sub-section 2 of section 436 cr.p.c empowers the court to refuse bail to an accused person even if the offence is bailable where the person granted bail fails to comply with the conditions of the bail bond.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 436-a cr.p.c:

page_content: deals with maximum period of detention of the under trial prisoner, where a person has undergone detention for a period extending up to one-half of the maximum period of imprisonment specified for that offence (not for the offence for which death punishment is specified), he shall be released by the court on his personal bond with or without sureties. it is done only after hearing the public prosecutor.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 436-a cr.p.c:

page_content: in case of bailable offence, the grant of bail is a matter of right. it may be either given by a police officer who is having the custody of accused or by the court.the accused may be released on bail, on executing a bond, known as 'bail bond', with or without furnishing sureties. the 'bail bond' may contain certain terms and conditions, such as:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 436-a cr.p.c:

page_content: - (a) the accused will not leave the territorial jurisdiction of the state without permission of court or police officer. - (b) the accused shall give his presence before police officer every time he is required to do so. - (c) the accused will not tamper with any evidence whatsoever, considered by police in the investigation. - (d) the court is empowered to refuse bail to an accused person even if the offence is bailable, where the person granted bail fails to comply with the conditions of the bail bond.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 437 cr.p.c:

page_content: when bail can be taken in non-bailable offences: when any person accused of, or suspected of, the commission of any non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police station or appears or is brought before a court other than the high court or court of session, he may be released on bail,

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 437 cr.p.c:

page_content: - (i) except the offences punishable with death or imprisonment for life - (ii) and such person who has previously been convicted of an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life or imprisonment for seven years or more

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 437 cr.p.c:

page_content: provided that the court may also direct 'that a person referred to in clause (i) or clause (ii) be released on bail if such person is under the age of sixteen years or is a woman or is sick or infirm.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 437 cr.p.c:

page_content: a non-bailable offence is one in which the grant of bail is not a matter of right. here the accused will have to apply to the court, and it will be the discretion of the court to grant bail or not. the application for bail shall be filed before the magistrate, who is conducting the trial. the application after being filed is usually listed on the next day. on such day, the application will be heard, and the police shall also present the accused in court. the magistrate may pass such orders, as he thinks fit. if the bail is granted, the accused will have to execute a 'bail bond'.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 437 cr.p.c:

page_content: on execution of bail-bond the accused is out of prison only on such terms and conditions, as contained in the 'bail-bond'. the amount of every bond, i.e. the security shall be reasonable and not excessive (sec 440). if, at any point of time, the terms and conditions of bail are not fulfilled, the 'bond' shall be forfeited. the application for bail shall be made in the form, prescribed and the designation of judge/magistrate should be clearly mentioned. the application shall also contain an undertaking, that the accused shall fulfill all the conditions as contained in the bail- bond.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: section 437 cr.p.c:

page_content: in non-bailable cases after forwarding the accused to the court and on filing the bail petition by the accused in the court, the io has to file counter by opposing the bail petition filed by the court by mentioning the grounds.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: grounds for opposing bail petition

page_content: 1. the accused may commit further offence 2. the accused may threaten the witnesses 3. the accused may try to tamper with the evidence 4. the accused may flee away from the jurisdiction 5. the accused may not cooperate with the investigating agency 6. there is likelihood of breach of peace and tranquility in the locality

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: grounds for opposing bail petition

page_content: 7. there is likelihood of retaliation by the victim party 8. the accused is required for police custody for recovery of the crime weapon. 9. the accused is required for police custody for recovery of stolen property. 10. the accused has to be sent to judicial custody for conducting of test identification parade. 11. if the accused is released on bail, the victim and the general public may loose confidence in the police as well as judiciary.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: anticipatory bail: (section 438 cr.p.c)

page_content: directions for grant of bail to person apprehending arrest:

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: anticipatory bail: (section 438 cr.p.c)

page_content: - (1) where any person has reason to believe that he may be arrested on accusation of having committed a non-bailable offence, he may apply to the high court or the court of session for a direction under this section that in the event of such arrest he shall be released on bail. the court may, after taking into consideration the following factors: 2. (i) the nature and gravity of the accusation; 3. (ii) the antecedents of the applicant including the fact as to whether he has previously undergone imprisonment on conviction by a court in respect of any cognizable offence; 4. (iii) the possibility of the applicant to flee from justice; 5. (iv) where the accusation has been made with the object of injuring or humiliating the applicant by having him so arrested, either reject the application forthwith or issue an interim order for the grant of anticipatory bail. - (2) when the high court or the court of session makes a direction under sub-section (1), the in charge of the police station shall not arrest as long as he complies with the conditions laid down in it.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: passport impounding and bail cancellation:

page_content: the police may have power to seize a passport under section 102 cr.p.c. if it is permissible within the authority given under section 102 of cr.p.c., it does not have power to retain or impound the same, because that can only be done by the passport authority under section 10(3) of the passports act. thereafter the police must send it along with a letter to the passport authority clearly stating that the seized passport deserves to be impounded for one of the reasons mentioned in section 10(3) of the act.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: passport impounding and bail cancellation:

page_content: it is, thereafter, for the passport authority to decide whether to impound the passport or not. since impounding of a passport has civil consequences, it does not have power to retain or impound the same, because that can only be done by the passport authority under section 10(3) of the passports act.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.filename: Investigation Theory, Practicals and Report Writing  Part -1.pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures
metadata.heading: passport impounding and bail cancellation:

page_content: the passports act is a special law while the cr.p.c. is a general law. it is well settled that the special law prevails over the general law. hence, impounding of a passport cannot be done by the court under section 104 cr.p.c though it can impound any other document or thing. the passport authority must give an opportunity of hearing to the person concerned before impounding his passport. ( state of orissa vs binapani dei [air 1967 sc 1269)
